content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95314

Technical Assistance Administration • • • Executive Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322 5660          322-5901          322 6441          322-6444

January 31, 1978

78-01-119

Senator Ruben Ayala
State Capitol
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Senator Ayala:

Thank you for your letter of January 5, 1978, in which you asked whether payments for food, lodging and transportation received from the Mexican government have to be reported on your Statement of Economic Interest, which is due no later than March 1. You also ask how to determine the value of such payments. It is our understanding that you were reimbursed for the transportation expenses by the Senate Rules Committee and that following the trip you issued a report outlining your findings.

The Political Reform Act requires each public official to list the sources of gifts of $25 or more on the Statement of Economic Interest. Section 87207.*
Under the Act, a gift is defined as "any payment to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received." Section 82028.

It appears that the trip was undertaken on behalf of the State of California, that you were functioning in your official capacity as a California legislator, and that the services you rendered were to the State of California and not to the Mexican government. We believe you received payments for which no consideration was provided and thus we feel that the payments you received from the Mexican government were gifts.

* All references are to the Government Code unless otherwise noted.

Senator Ruben Ayala
Page Two                                January 31, 1978


     You ask whether you must make inquiries to the Mexican
government to determine the value of the gifts.

     The Act requires that you use all reasonable diligence
in the preparation of your Statement of Economic Interest.
However, it is not necessary to make inquiries to the source
of the gift.  We believe that if you can make a good faith
estimate of the value of the gift that you have met the
above standard.  Of course, if you would rather make
inquiries of the donor, that is permissible.

     If you have any further questions, please feel free
to call me.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Robert M. Stern
                                        General Counsel

REPLY TO:
LEGISLATIVE ADDRESS
STATE CAPITOL—ROOM 2048
SACRAMENTO, CA 95814
(916) 445-6968                                                                    COMMITTEES
                                                                                  AGRICULTURE AND
DISTRICT OFFICE ADDRESSES                                                        WATER RESOURCES,
                                                                                  CHAIRMAN
323 WEST 6TH STREET                                                              LOCAL GOVERNMENT
SUITE 103                                                                        NATURAL RESOURCES AND
SAN BERNARDINO, CA 92401                                                         WILDLIFE
(714) 884-3165                                                                   REVENUE AND TAXATION

1603 WEST 6TH STREET
SUITE 102
ONTARIO, CA 91762
(714) 983-3566

                                            California State Senate

                                                    January 5, 1978




Dr. Daniel H. Lowenstein, Chairman
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Dr. Lowenstein:

     The Agriculture and Water Resources Committee
visited Mexico during the week of October 17, 1977 at
the invitation of the People of Mexico to discuss the
exportation of Mexican agricultural products to
California.

     The Members of the Committee paid for their own
air fare to Mexico, but the Mexican Government supplied
our food, lodging, and transportation while in Mexico.
Should we ask them how much they spent on each of us
and report it, or does this have to be reported?

     There is a certain amount of urgency on this
opinion since many of the Members are preparing their
year-end reports. I would appreciate hearing from you
soon.

     Thank you.

                                            Sincerely,


                                            RUBEN S. AYALA
                                            State Senator

WILDLIFE, VICE CHAIRMAN
REVENUE AND TAXATION

STATE SENATOR

RUBEN S. AYALA
THIRTY-SECOND SENATORIAL DISTRICT

California State Senate

January 25, 1978


The Honorable Daniel H. Lowenstein
Chairman, Fair Political Practices
Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California

Dear Dr. Lowenstein:

This is in reference to my January 5, 1978 letter
(copy enclosed) asking for an opinion on the reporting
of the trip to Mexico by the Senate Agriculture and
Water Resources Committee.

We need an answer from you in order to meet the
deadline on reporting. Would you be kind enough to
send me your response immediately.

Thank you.

Sincerely,

RUBEN S. AYALA
State Senator

Dear Dr. Lowenstein:

The Agriculture and Water Resources Committee
visited Mexico during the week of October 17, 1977 at
the invitation of the people of Mexico to discuss the
exportation of Mexican agricultural products to
California.

The Members of the Committee paid for their own
air fare to Mexico, but the Mexican Government supplied
our food, lodging, and transportation while in Mexico.
Should we ask them how much they spent on each of us
and report it, or does this have to be reported?

There is a certain amount of urgency on this
opinion since many of the Members are preparing their
year-end reports. I would appreciate hearing from you
soon.

Thank you.

Sincerely,

RUBEN S. AYALA

sections.conclusion: You ask whether you must make inquiries to the source of the gift to determine the value of what you use to report on your Statement of Economic Interest. However, it is not necessary to make inquiries to the source of the gift. We believe that if you can make a good faith estimate of the value of the gift that should be reported, you will have met the standard. Of course, if you would rather make inquiry of the donor, that is permissible.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether California State Senators must inquire about and report the value of food, lodging, and transportation provided by the Mexican Government during an official committee visit to Mexico, when the senators paid for their own airfare.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advises that while senators may inquire about the value of gifts from foreign governments, they are not required to do so; instead, they must report a good faith estimate of the value on their Statement of Economic Interest.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: OCR document with moderate quality. FPPC response appears to be the upper portion of the document (response letter from FPPC). Multiple versions/copies of the incoming request included. Some text degradation due to OCR but question and conclusion sections are sufficiently legible for extraction.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to California State Senator Ruben Ayala regarding disclosure requirements for gifts of food, lodging, and transportation provided by the Mexican Government during an official committee visit in October 1977.
